ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for grape-and-aloe flavoured milk; closest defensible match is 'flavoured milk beverage' (FOODON_03301737). Grape and aloe vera are added flavouring and texture components.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flavoured milk-based drinks) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for grape-aloe milk drinks.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific flavoured milk beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one cup or bottle as typically served at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Whole milk, fluid, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy); Grapes, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy); Aloe vera juice, drink (USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Grape with Aloe Milk' as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of: whole milk (~60% by volume, USDA FDC #746782), grape juice (~30%, USDA FDC #167771), and aloe vera gel/bits (~10%, USDA FDC #2003571). Proportions are inferred from typical Japanese-style flavoured milk drinks. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — grape juice and milk composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Grape with Aloe Milk. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous foods: grape juice GI ~52 (University of Sydney GI Database), whole milk GI ~27 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted blend (~60% milk, ~30% grape juice, ~10% aloe vera) yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 55. Aloe vera bits contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 11.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 15.8, rounded to 15.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~32 mg per 100 g; ~80 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 80 mg sodium per serving, Grape with Aloe Milk is a low-sodium beverage suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the natural sugars from grape juice should be noted if consumed frequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but moderate sugar content (~10.8 g per 100 g; ~27 g per 250 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: While Grape with Aloe Milk has a low GI of approximately 55, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should still consume it in moderation due to its approximately 27 g of sugar per standard 250 ml serving, which can impact blood glucose levels; portion control and awareness of total carbohydrate intake are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g) and low sodium; grape polyphenols may offer mild cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grape with Aloe Milk is relatively low in saturated fat and sodium, and the grape component provides polyphenols with potential mild cardioprotective properties; however, the added sugar content warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease managing weight or triglyceride levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grape with Aloe Milk is a Japanese-style flavoured milk beverage served at casual Japanese dining establishments in Singapore, notably at Sukiya outlets. It combines the sweet, fruity aroma of grape juice or grape flavouring with chewy aloe vera bits and the rich, creamy character of Hokkaido milk — a premium full-fat milk from Japan's Hokkaido prefecture prized for its natural sweetness and smooth texture. The drink is typically served chilled and has a light purple or pinkish hue. The texture is predominantly smooth and milky, punctuated by soft, translucent aloe vera gel pieces that add a mild, slightly herbal note and a pleasant chewiness. Nutritionally, the beverage provides moderate energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), a modest protein contribution from the milk (~2.2 g per 100 g), and a relatively high sugar content (~10.8 g per 100 g) primarily from the grape component. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), making it a beverage that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume with awareness of its sugar content. The sodium content is low (~32 mg per 100 g), making it broadly suitable for those managing hypertension. Calcium from the Hokkaido milk (~78 mg per 100 g) supports bone health. The drink is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, but contains dairy and is not appropriate for lactose-intolerant or vegan consumers. It is typically priced at SGD 5.80 per serving in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Grape with Aloe Milk' exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for whole milk, grape juice, and aloe vera. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database values for milk and grape juice. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific commercial product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for whole milk (~60%), grape juice (~30%), and aloe vera (~10%) by volume. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific product was available from any approved source. 2. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for grape juice (GI ~52) and whole milk (GI ~27), weighted by approximate volume proportions. This GI of 55 is categorized as 'low'. Aloe vera bits contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 11.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 15.8, rounded to 15.9. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the constituent ingredients at these proportions — confirmed zero. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy milk and grape juice profiles. 5. All micronutrient values are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy composite and should be treated as approximate. 6. The food is served at Sukiya (Funan) and Funan Mix & Match in Singapore at SGD 5.80. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 (flavoured milk beverage) and FoodEx2 A00LC (Flavoured milk-based drinks) are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific product. 8. No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero. 9. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all micronutrient -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the USDA composite, so no -1 sentinel documentation is required.
